coredata.dc:title: Efficient one step synthesis of green carbon quantum dots catalyzed by tin oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Xiong, Hui-Fang
An, Bao-Li
Zhang, Ji-Ming
Yin, Chang-ling
Wang, Xiao-Hong
Wang, Jia-Hui
Xu, Jia-Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) have been synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with isopropanol as carbon source. The color of BCQDs was tuned to be green by incorporating N atoms into BCQDs with m-phenylenediamine as nitrogen atom source. Strong green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) have been obtained, and the pyrolysis reaction time was reduced much by using SnCl5⋅5H2O as a catalyst. The quantum yield for the GCQDs reached 36 % in one hour, and the quantum yield reached 55 % in 4 h. Moreover, the fluorescence of GCQDs could be selectively quenched by NO2 − ions, and the relative fluorescence intensities of I/I0 was fitted perfectly in line decay curve versus the concentration of NO2 − in the range of 1.0 × 10−6 mol/l–1.6 × 10−5 mol/l (R2 = 0.9998).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Luminescence
Catalysis
Sensor
Nitrite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of blue carbon quantum dots
Synthesis of green carbon quantum dots
The morphology and composition of GCQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge financial support from the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Chemistry , Shanghai Institute of Organic Chemistry , Chinese Academy of Sciences , The Shanghai Natural Science Foundation ( 19ZR1418900 ) and the Innovation Project for College Students of Shanghai University from Shanghai Government , National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2017YFB0102900 ), Chinese Academy of Sciences ( KLIFMD201704 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61671284 ; U1704255 ), and the Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Peak Discipline Construction program), the Innovation Project for College Students of Shanghai University from Shanghai Government. The authors also acknowledge the Instrumental Analysis and Research Center of Shanghai University for providing measurement services.

We acknowledge financial support from the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Chemistry, Shanghai Institute of Organic Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, The Shanghai Natural Science Foundation (19ZR1418900) and the Innovation Project for College Students of Shanghai University from Shanghai Government, National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0102900), Chinese Academy of Sciences (KLIFMD201704), National Natural Science Foundation of China (61671284; U1704255), and the Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Peak Discipline Construction program), the Innovation Project for College Students of Shanghai University from Shanghai Government. The authors also acknowledge the Instrumental Analysis and Research Center of Shanghai University for providing measurement services.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the GCQD 4 h. (a) The inserted chart is the size distribution chart for GCQD 4 h, (b) the inserted image is the high resolution image for GCQD 4 h.

a) The high resolution XP spectrum of C1 s; (b) the high resolution XP spectrum of N1 s; (c) the XPS full survey spectrum for GCQD 4 h; (d) the Roman spectrum for GCQD 4 h, (e) the XRD pattern for GCQD 4 h.
The proposed mechanism for synthesis of the CQDs.
UV absorption spectra of BCQDs and GCQDs in isopropanol dilute solutions.
The excitation and emission spectra for BCQDs and GCQDs in dilute isopropanol solutions.
Lifetime decay curve for GCQD 6 h at 500 nm.
a)Time-dependent PL spectra of GCQD 4 h quenched by 1.0 × 10−4 mol/l NO2 −, (b) Time-dependent relative PL maximum peak intensity of GCQD 4 h aqueous solution versus the time.

a) PL spectra of GCQD 4 h with different concentrations of NO2 − excited at 380 nm, (b) The relative maximum peak intensity curve versus the concentrations of NO2 − in the range of 1.0 × 10−6 mol/l ∼ 1.0 × 10-4 mol/l. (c) The I0 / I versus the concentrations of NO2 −.
The relative fluorescence intensity I / I 0 versus the concentration of NO2 − in the range of 1.0 × 10-6 mol/l ∼ 1.6 × 10-5 mol/l.

a) Relative PL peak intensities I / I0 of GCQD 4 h aqueous solutions with different anions, [all anions] = 1.0 × 10−4 mol/l. (b) Relative peak intensities of (I0 – I mixture) / I0 for GCQD 4 h aqueous solutions containing different anions. (c) Interference tests of common anions on relative PL peak intensities of GCQD 4 h aqueous solutions, [NO2 −] = 1.0 × 10-5 mol/l, [other interference anions] = 1.0 × 10−4 mol/l. (d) Relative error caused by different interference anions.

a) Relative PL peak intensities I mixture / I0 of GCQD 4 h aqueous solutions containing different cations, [NO2 −] = 1.0 × 10-5 mol/l, [all cations] = 1.0 × 10-4 mol/l. (b) Relative peak intensities of (I 0 – I mixture) /I0 for GCQD 4 h aqueous solutions containing different cations. (c) Interference tests of common cations on relative PL peak intensities of GCQD 4 h aqueous solutions, [NO2 −] = 1.0 × 10-5 mol/l, [other interference cations] = 1.0 × 10-4 mol/l. (d) Relative error caused by different interference cations.
The PLQYs for GCQDs synthesized at different reaction times.

head.ce:title: Efficient one step synthesis of green carbon quantum dots catalyzed by tin oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Periodicals Agency, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Strong green carbon dots were synthesized in one step sovolthermal process with common chemicals, the synthesis time was greatly reduced by using nano tin oxide particls as catalysts.

Blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) have been synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with isopropanol as carbon source. The color of BCQDs was tuned to be green by incorporating N atoms into BCQDs with m-phenylenediamine as nitrogen atom source. Strong green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) have been obtained, and the pyrolysis reaction time was reduced much by using SnCl5⋅5H2O as a catalyst. The quantum yield for the GCQDs reached 36 % in one hour, and the quantum yield reached 55 % in 4 h. Moreover, the fluorescence of GCQDs could be selectively quenched by NO2 − ions, and the relative fluorescence intensities of I/I0 was fitted perfectly in line decay curve versus the concentration of NO2 − in the range of 1.0 × 10−6 mol/l–1.6 × 10−5 mol/l (R2 = 0.9998).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-spherical nanomaterials with particle size less than 10 nm [1]. CQDs have received great attention duo to their excellent properties in terms of high fluorescent efficiency, stable luminescence, low toxicity to biomolecules and low cost [2–4], and they have been extensively used in bioimaging, luminescent molecular labelling, luminescent molecular sensors, etc [3,5]. The luminescent mechanism for CQDs is very complicated, and two luminescent mechanisms have been accepted by most of scientists [6]. The one mechanism is that the band gap states come from the conjugated -domains of the CQDs, and the other is that the fluorescence emission comes from the surface defects of the CQDs [7–9]. The regulation of the optical color of CQDs is important for understanding the luminescence mechanism of CQDs. Professor Sun Zaicheng's group obtained blue, green and red color luminescence by controlling the graphitization degree of the CQDs and decoration of functional groups on the surface of the CQDs using citric acid and urea as the raw chemicals [10]. Blue and green-yellow color emissions from CQDs were obtained by adjusting the degree of the carbonization and the conjugate structure in the CQDs [11]. Sun and co-workers reported blue, green and yellow color emissions of CQDs by incorporating N atoms in the graphene quantum dots and adjusting the conjugated structure in different solvent [12].

There are many methods to synthesize carbon quantum dots, such as hydrothermal method, microwave method and so on [13,14]. Microwave irradiation and microwave plasma are efficient and time-saving methods, however, the CQDs prepared by microwave irradiation and microwave plasma methods generally have low luminescent quantum efficiency with inhomogeneous particles [15,16]. Hydrothermal method is extensively used to synthesize CQDs which showed good luminescent properties together with good luminescent stability in air [17]. At present, it generally need long reaction time to synthesize CQDs using hydrothermal method, sometimes the CQDs still showed low luminescent quantum efficiency. Green CQDs were successively synthesized by hydrothermal method with phenylenediamine as the source of N atoms which were incorporated in the CQDs to regulate the color, but it needed 12 h and the luminescent quantum yield was only 17.6 % [18]. The quantum yield for green CQDs has been increased to 90 % by hydrothermal treatment of rose bengal and branched polyethylenimine [19]. Most sulfur-containing chemicals generally have unpleasant odor, they are harmful to people's health. It still has great significance to find an environmental friendly method for synthesizing luminescent CQDs with good properties. It is also meaningful to reduce the cost for the extensive application of CQDs by shortening the reaction time.

Nitrite is widely used in foods as a preservative, and the level of nitrite in foods and water have been greatly concerned. Excess nitrite can form toxic and carcinogenic nitrosamines, which is very unhealthy for health [20]. It is of great significance to detect the concentration of nitrite for environmental monitoring and human health in today [21]. So far, many methods have been reported to detect nitrite, such as chromatography, microflow injection analysis, etc, and these methods are generally complex and time consuming, requiring cumbersome sample pretreatment [22,23]. Fluorescence sensor is still a simple, sensitive method to detect nitrite.

Here, green CQDs (GCQDs) with good luminescent properties have been synthesized in one step reaction. The synthesis time was reduced greatly by using a mental compound as a catalyst. This synthetic pathway has never been reported before. The quantum yield for the GCQDs reached 36 % in one hour, and the maximum quantum yield reached 55 % in four hours. As the luminescent intensity of the GCQDs was declined linearly with the concentration of nitrate in range of 1.0 × 10−6 mol/l - 1.6 × 10−5 mol/l, a simple, selective fluorescence sensor of NO2 − has been established. The analytical detection limit is 1.0 × 10−6 mol/l. The effects of common interfering ions, including common anions and cations in water and food, were investigated in detail, and the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was proposed and discussed.

Isopropyl alcohol (HPLC grade), m-phenylenediamine (99.5 %), tin(IV) chloride pentahydrate (99.0 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. of China. NaNO2, NaNO3, NaI, NaBr, NaF, Na3PO4, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, Na2SO3, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, Na2SO4, NaCl, MgCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, KCl, NiCl2·6H2O, AgNO3, CaCl2, ZnCl2 and PbCl2 are all analytical grade, and they were used directly without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of blue carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Blue carbon quantum dots were synthesized by an environmentally friendly hydrothermal method. A typical experiment is as follows. 0.3506 g SnCl4·5H2O (0.100 mmol) and 0.2925 g n-butylamine (0.400 mmol) were dissolved in 25 mL isopropyl alcohol solution. Hydrolysis of SnCl4·5H2O were completed with equivalent reaction of n-butylamine, and nano tin oxide particle solution was obtained by the hydrolysis reaction at 60 °C for 1 h. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, and it was kept at 180 °C for 1 h. The resulting solution was cooled naturally to room temperature. Pyrolysis of isopropanol was efficiently catalyzed by the nano tin oxide particles, the tin oxide particles could be easily removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the solution was filtered under vacuum with 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the larger nano carbon particles, and the soluble small molecules were removed by dialysis (molecular weight 3500 Da) in the dialysis bag. Bright blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (BCQD 1 h) were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of green carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Green carbon quantum dots were synthesized by similar method as that for BCQDs just replacing n-butylamine by 0.200 mmol m-phenylenediamine. The mixed solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, and it was kept at 180 °C for 1–8 h. Pyrolysis of isopropanol and m-phenylenediamine was efficiently catalyzed by the nano tin oxide particles, the tin oxide particles were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the solution was filtered under vacuum with 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the larger nano carbon particles, and the soluble small molecules were removed by dialysis (molecular weight 3500 Da) in the dialysis bag. High efficient green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (named as GCQD 1 h, GCQD 2 h, GCQD 4 h, GCQD 6 h, GCQD 8 h respectively) doped with nitrogen atoms were obtained. The concentration of GCQD 4 h was ca. 22 mg/mL.

The detection of NO2 − was carried out at room temperature (25 °C) in 0.020 M PBS buffer solution (pH = 4.0). In a typical experiment, 10 mL of synthesized GCQD 4 h solution with a concentration of 22 mg/ml was diluted to a total volume of 100 mL by 0.020 mol/l PBS solution. Then, 1.0 × 10−6mol/l ∼ 1.0 × 10−4 mol/l concentration of NO2- was added into 3.0 mL diluted GCQD 4 h solution at room temperature. After 10 min placement of the samples, the emission spectra for the resulting samples were recorded under excitation at 380 nm with same parameters of the fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Selectivity experiments for GCQD 4 h solutions containing different anions were carried out in 0.020 mol/l PBS buffer solution (pH = 4.0) at room temperature, the concentration of all the investigated anions was 1.0 × 10−4mol/l, In a typical experimet, 1.0 × 10−4 mol/l aqueous solutions of NO3-, I-, Br-, F-, PO4 3-, H2PO4−, HPO4 2-, SO3 2-, CO3 2-, HCO3−, SO4 2-, NO2− were mixed with 3.00 mL diluted GCQD 4 h solution respectively, and the emission spectra were recorded after 10 min placement of the samples. The emission spectra for 1.0 10−4 mol/l aqueous solutions of Cu2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Na+ and K+ were also obtained with the same method as described for the anion samples.

The interference tests for the above mentioned anions and cations on the relative PL intensities of the GCQD 4 h under coexistence of the each interference ion and NO2 −. When mixed ions were tested, the concentrations for NO2 − and each interfering ion were 1.0 10-5 mol/l and 1.0 10-4 mol/l respectively. The concentration ratio of NO2 − versus each interfering ion (anions and cations) was 1:10. The test conditions were carried out as the same as those for individual ion tests.

The morphology and size of the carbon quantum dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM-2010 F). The test condition was carried out at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 with an Al anode as Kα source. The XRD analysis was carried out with a powder diffractometer (DX2700) using CuKa radiation (λ =1.54184 Å). Raman spectrum was performed on a laser Raman spectrometer (Renishaw in Via plus) with a 633 nm laser as the excitation source light. The infrared spectra in the range of 4000−400 cm−1 were measured on a Thermo Nicolet AVATRA 370 infrared spectrophotometer by conventional KBr method. The ultraviolet-visible spectra were recorded on an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Pulsing TU-1950) at room temperature. The excitation and emission spectra were measured by a transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLS 1000) with both the excitation slit and the emission slit of 0.5 nm. The lifetimes for the BCQDs and GCQDs were obtained by auto calculation of the time decay curves which were recorded from Edinburgh Instrument of FLS 1000 with EPL-Visible 450 nm laser (10-12 s) as the excitation source light. The absolute quantum yields were also determined on Edinburgh Instrument of FLS 1000 with a Red PMT sphere, all the quantum yield values were obtained by auto calculation by this instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and composition of GCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images indicate that the size of the synthesized GCQDs mainly ranged from 2 to 3 nm. The high resolution image for GCQD 4 h is given in the inserted image of Fig. 1 (b), and the in-plane lattice spacing of the GCQD 4 h is 0.21 nm. Moreover, the GCQDs could be uniformly dispersed in water solution or some organic solvents such as ethanol, Isopropyl alcohol (Fig. 1).

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra for the GCQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . Strong absorption peaks at 2980 cm−1 and 2886 cm−1 were attributed respectively to νas, C
H (CH3) and νs, C
H (CH3), and the medium absorption peak at 1464 cm−1 (δas, C
H (CH3)) and strong absorption peak at 1385 cm−1 (δs, C
H (CH3)) further confirmed the existence of CH3 groups. Two weak narrow peak as 3670 cm−1 (νas, N-H) and 3645 cm−1 (νs, N-H ) were attributed to characteristic absorptions of NH2 groups, weak absorption peak at 1550 cm−1 (δN
H, in plane) and medium absorption at 819 cm−1 (δN
H, out of the plane) further confirmed the existence of NH2 groups. The wavenumber for the stretch vibration absorption of CN is close to that for CC bond, and the stretch vibration absorption peaks for both CN and CC bonds shift to larger wavenumbers as they bond conjugated structure [24,25]. CN is a polar bond and CC is a nonpolar bond, the absorption peaks for CN bonds are generally stronger than those for CC bonds. Moreover, the wavenumber of CN bond generally lesser than that of CC bonds. Thus, peaks at 1154 cm−1 and 951 cm−1 were attributed to vibration absorption of CN bonds, and peaks at 1252 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1 were ascribed to vibration absorption of CC bonds. Fig. 2 showed that there were two kinds of bonds for both CC and CN. However, characteristic absorption peaks for CC, OH, CO and COOH groups were not observed in Fig. 2. While, the characteristic absorption peaks for NH2 group were not found in the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra for the BCQDs, the reason was ascribed to that the pyrolysis of n-butylamine might not happened in the reaction condition due to the stable linear chain structure n-butylamine. Characteristic absorption peaks of CH3 group visibly showed in the IR spectrum for BCQDs (see the supporting information, Fig. S1).

The XPS survey spectrum for GCQD 4h showed characteristic peaks of C 1s (284.1 eV), N 1s (398.7 eV), Cl 2p (197.6 eV), Cl 2s (268.5 eV) (Fig. 3 (c)). As the sample was coated on silicon wafer, characteristic peaks of Si 2p (101.8 eV), Si 2s (152.6 eV) and O 1s (529.7 eV) was found. Peak for C 1s was fitted two peaks of CC (284.1 eV) and CN (285.2 eV) respectively (Fig. 3(a)) [26,27]. Two peaks for N 1s were attributed to characteristic peaks of pyridine N (398.7 eV) and NH (401.0 eV) respectively (Fig. 3(b)) [28,29]. The XRD pattern of the as-prepared GCQD 4 h (Fig. 3(e)) shows a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 25.9°, which is attributed to the (002) diffraction patterns of the graphitic carbon with bulk graphite (0.335 nm). The Raman spectrum for GCQD 4 h is showed in Fig. 3(d), and the peaks centered at ca. 1327 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1 are attributed to the D and G bands of carbon materials respectively. The ID /IG ratio is ca. 1.1, which is close to that of the original graphene film (∼1.05) [30].

BCQDs were obtained by the complicated pyrolysis reaction of isopropanol as carbon source with nano tin oxide as a catalyst, and the surface group of BCQDs was testified to be methyl. The surface of GCQDs was affirmed to have both methyl and amino groups by the IR and XPS data. The synthesis mechanism for BCQDs and GCQDs was proposed in Fig. 4 .

As shown in Fig. 5 , the absorption peaks for BCQDs located at 231 nm, 277 nm–298 nm and 353 nm. The absorption peaks for the GCQDs shifted to longer wavelengths compared with those for the BCQDs. The wavelength at around 290 nm for the GCQDs shifted slightly to longer wavelength with the increased synthesis time from 2 h to 8 h. The weak absorption bands in region of 409 nm–462 nm were ascribed to the absorption of different surface states of the GCQDs [26].

Bright blue fluorescence was observed for BCQD 1 h under ultraviolet radiation. The maximum excitation wavelength for BCQD 1 h is 393 nm, and its maximum emission wavelength is 465 nm (Fig. 6 ). As the n-butylamine was replaced by 1,3-diaminebenzene, green fluorescence for GCQDs was observed. The maximum emission wavelength for GCQDs shifted 35 nm to longer wavelength compared with that for BCQD 1 h. The emission intensity for GCQDs reached the maximum in 4 h, then, the emission intensity declined slightly for GCQD 8 h. Moreover, the intensity for excitation peak at 464 nm reached the maximum in 4 h, and the intensity for excitation peak at 409 nm reached the maximum in 6 h. The visible excitation absorption bands were ascribed to the surface states of GCQDs, so two surface states were formed in the hydrothermal synthesis process. Interestingly, the intensity for the narrow excitation peak at 270 nm declined with synthesis time from 1 h to 8 h. However, the maximum emission wavelength did not shift to longer wavelength with the increased synthesis time in 8 h, this phenomenon showed that the GCQDs were not semicondunctor and their emission color depended on the surface states formed in the hydrothermal synthesis process. In addition, the maximum emission wavelength shifted around 5 nm under different excitation wavelength (see Supporting information Fig. S2).

The absolute photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) were determined at the same conditions. The PLQY for GCQD 1 h was determined to be 36.3 0.3 %, and the PLQY for GCQDs reached the maximum in 4 h. Then the PLQY declined slightly as the synthesis time over 4 h (Table 1 ).

The lifetime for BCQD 1 h was calculated from the emission decay curve at 465 nm excited by an EPL-UV 375 nm laser (10−12 s), and The lifetime for GCQDs were calculated from the emission decay curves at 500 nm excited by an EPL-visible 450 nm laser (10−12 s). All the lifetime values were obtained by the auto calculation of FLS 1000. All the decay curves are fitted perfectly in second exponential decay equation, and the results are given in Table 2 . The lifetime for GCQD 6 h reached the maximum, and the average lifetime is 8.9 0.1 ns. The decay curve for GCQD 6 h is given in Fig. 7 , and other decay curves are given in the supporting information of Figs. S3–S7.

In 0.020 mol/l aqueous PBS buffer solution (pH = 4.0), the fluorescence of the GCQDs was quenched efficiently by NO2 − ions. Moreover, the fluorescent intensity of GCQDs can be invisibly decreased under the presence of 1.0 10−6 mol/l NO2 −, indicating the sensible detection of NO2 − with GCQDs as a fluorescent agent.

In pH = 4.0 aqueous solution, the fluorescence of GCQD was quenched quickly by 1.0 10−4 mol/l NO2 −. Fig. 8 (a) showed the time-dependent PL spectra of GCQD 4 h quenched by 1.0 10−4 mol/l NO2 −, the spectra were recorded once every 2 min. However, the maximum wavelength shifted from 500 nm to 531 nm in 40 min after addition of NO2- ions. This phenomenon indicated that the NO2- might be reacted with the surface groups of GCQDs, and new surface states might be formed with the fluorescence color change. In Fig. 8(b), I0 is the maximum intensity of GCQD 4 h in pH = 4.0 PBS aqueous solution, the relative intensity I/I0 was fitted perfectly in the second exponentially decay curve (red line in Fig. 8(b)) versus the time. The equation is given in Eq. (1).
(1)
I
I 0
=
A 0
+
A 1
e
-
t + 0.83383
t 1
+
A 2
e
-
t + 0.83383
t 2
Here, A0 = 0.2288 0.0009, A1 = 1.25053 0, A2 = 0.44511 0, t1 = 0.76 0.11, t2 = 3.69 0.02. The equation showed that the fluorescence intensity decreased quickly in 10 min, then, the fluorescence decreased slowly in the second step. The fluorescence intensity remained approximately invariance after 15 min. For better reproducibility, the PL intensities for all the samples added different concentrations of NO2 − were determined after 15 min in the following experiments.

The PL spectra of aqueous GCQD 4 h with different concentrations of NO2 − (1.0 10−6 mol/l ∼ 1.0 10-4 mol/l) are given in Fig. 9 (a) excited at 380 nm, and all the spectra were recorded after 10 min as the each NO2- solution wad mixed with the same GCQD 4 h solutions. The maximum emission wavelength shifted to 531 nm, it was consistent with the data in Fig. 8(a). As different concentrations of NO2- were added into the aqueous GCQD 4 h, the color of the solutions changed obviously. The fluorescence color changed from green to yellow-green under radiation of 365 nm and the intensity obviously weakened (Supporting information Fig. S8). The relative fluorescence intensities, I/I0 , was fitted good in the first exponentially decay curve versus the concentration of NO2- ions in the range of 1.0 × 10−6 mol/l–1.0 10-4 mol/l (red line in Fig. 9(b)). The equation is given in Eq. (2).
(2)
I
I 0
=
B 0
+
B 1
e
-
c + 1.5516
t 1
Here, B0 = 0.25 0.02, B 1 = 0.78779 0, t1 = 24 2.

The data in Fig. 9(b) were analyzed by the Stern-Volmer equation (I0 / I = 1 + Ksv [Q]) to investigate the fluorescence quench mechanism [31,32]. Here, I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities without and with a quencher, respectively, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher, Ksv is the kinetic quenching constant. Fig. 9(c) shows that the data are fitted well in the Stern-Volmer equation (Ksv = 0.0379 ± 0.0007 l mol−1, R2 = 0.998, room temperature).

Interestingly, the I / I0 was fitted perfectly in line decay curve versus the concentration of NO2 − ions in the range of 1.0 10-6 mol/l–1.6 10-5 mol/l (Fig. 10 ). The equation is given in Eq. (3).
(3)
I/I0 = a + b c
Here, a = 0.960 0.002, b = −0.0222 0.0002, R2 = 0.9998.

The maximum limit value of 2.2 10−6 mol/l (0.1 mg l-1) NO2- in drinking water was regulated by the European Community [33], the Joint Food Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) and the World Health Organization (WHO) Expert Committee stipulates that the nitrite in dairy foods should not exceed 4.3 10−6 mol/l (0.2 mg l-1) [34]. In our experiments, the actual detection limit of 1.0 10−6 mol/l was obtained, and it was promising for sensitive detection of NO2- ions in drinking water and food.

Fig. 11 gives the selectivity experiment results for GCQD 4 h in aqueous solutions containing different anions. As shown Fig. 11(a), a much lower relative PL intensity was observed for GCQD 4 h upon addition of NO2 − ions, while no significant decrease of the relative fluorescence intensity was found in the presence of other anions except for IO3 −. As shown in Fig. 11(b), the relative (I 0 - I mixture) / I 0 for NO2 − was 68.3 %, the value for IO3 − was 23.8 %, and the values for other interference anions were below 3.0 %. In addition, interference tests under coexistence conditions were also completed (Fig. 11(c)), the relative errors caused by different interference anions were given in Fig. 11(d). All the interference test experiments were carried out under competitive quenching conditions with the concentration ratio of 1: 10 for NO2 − versus each interfering ion. The data further confirmed the relative error caused by IO3 − is 23.8 %. However, IO3 − is an important interference anion for selective fluorescence detection of NO2 − in water or food.

Fig. 12 gives the selectivity experiment results for GCQD 4 h in aqueous solutions containing different cations. As shown Fig. 12(a), no significant decrease of the relative fluorescence intensity was found in the presence of other common cations. As shown in Fig. 12(b), the relative (I0 - I mixture) / I0 for other interference cations were all below 2.3 %. In addition, interference tests of cations under coexistence conditions were also completed (Fig. 12(c)), the relative errors caused by different interference cations were given in Fig. 12(d). All the interference test experiments were carried out under competitive quenching conditions with the concentration ratio of 1:10 for NO2 − versus each interfering cation. The relative errors caused by Fe2+, Ag+, Ni2+ and K+ were 11.4 %, 4.9 %, 4.7 % and 3.2 % respectively, and the relative errors for other cations were all bellow 2.9 %. However, Fe2+ is a visible interference cation for selective fluorescence detection of NO2 − in water, the relative error for other cations are all bellow 5%. The interfering effect reason for Fe2+ was ascribed to the redox reaction between Fe2+ and NO2 − ions [35], and the interfering reason for Ag+ was attributed to the excitation absorption of AgNO2 complex formed in aqueous solution [36]. Ni2+ ions can be coordinated by some NO2 − ions [37], so ten times concentration of Ni2+ slightly interfaces the selective detention of nitrite.

Green luminescent carbon quantum dots have been synthesized with isopropanol used as carbon source and m-phenylenediamine as nitrogen source. Compared to that of the literatures, the synthesis time was reduced much by tin oxide nano particles as a catalyst. The quantum efficiency of the synthesized green carbon quantum dots reached 36.3 % in one hour, and the highest quantum yield of 55.8 % was obtained in 4 h. The fluorescence of the synthesized green CQDs was decreased selectively by NO2 − ions, they could be used for selective determination of NO2 − in water or food with a actual detection limit of 1.0 × 10-6 mol/l. This synthesis method can be carried out in air without complicated equipment. High efficient green CQDs can be synthesized in hours, and it is helpful for extensive application of CQDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Chemistry, Shanghai Institute of Organic Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, The Shanghai Natural Science Foundation (19ZR1418900) and the Innovation Project for College Students of Shanghai University from Shanghai Government, National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0102900), Chinese Academy of Sciences (KLIFMD201704), National Natural Science Foundation of China (61671284; U1704255), and the Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Peak Discipline Construction program), the Innovation Project for College Students of Shanghai University from Shanghai Government. The authors also acknowledge the Instrumental Analysis and Research Center of Shanghai University for providing measurement services.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2020.101762.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
